verses.text: Eliphaz claims the foolish man takes root only to be suddenly cursed, his harvest devoured and his children crushed. He paints the swift ruin of the wicked. He means Job to see himself in it, pressing his theory upon a grief it does not fit.

Affliction, he says, does not spring from the dust, for 'man is born unto trouble, as the sparks fly upward.' Here he speaks truly: trouble is the common lot of fallen man. Yet a true saying becomes unkind when used only to condemn.

Affliction, he says, does not spring from the dust, for 'man is born unto trouble, as the sparks fly upward.' Here he speaks truly: trouble is the common lot of fallen man. Yet a true saying becomes unkind when used only to condemn.

'I would seek unto God,' says Eliphaz, who does great and unsearchable things, sending rain and raising the lowly. The counsel to seek God is good. The wonder is that he urges on Job, as if new, what Job has done all his life.

God, he says, foils the crafty, saves the poor from their mouth, 'so the poor hath hope.' God's care for the weak is real comfort. Even a mistaken friend may speak truth that another day will prove sweet.

'Happy is the man whom God correcteth,' for He wounds and binds up, smites and heals. Here Eliphaz touches gold: chastening is a mercy. God's blows on His children are the strokes of a Father, meant to heal, not to destroy.

He promises deliverance in famine and war, safety from the scourging tongue, and peace even with the beasts and stones of the field. He pledges blessings he cannot guarantee. God's care is sure, but men err who promise its outcomes on their own terms.

Job's home shall be at peace, his offspring many, and he shall come to the grave 'in a full age,' Eliphaz concludes — 'we have searched it, so it is.' He is confident of his system. But God will soon show that the wise have not searched out His ways.
